question: List and briefly describe the three primary steps involved in mapping a simple, strong entity type from an ER diagram to a relational schema.
expectedPoints: Identify the entity type and create a new relation (table) for it.
Map each simple attribute of the entity type to an attribute (column) in the relation.
Choose one of the key attributes to serve as the primary key for the relation.

question: Explain the process for mapping a binary One-to-Many (1:N) relationship from an ER diagram to the relational model. Include in your answer where the foreign key is placed and why.
expectedPoints: The entity on the 'Many' (N) side of the relationship becomes the referencing relation.
A foreign key attribute is added to this relation that corresponds to the primary key of the entity on the 'One' (1) side.
This approach is used because it avoids creating a separate table for the relationship, which is necessary for a Many-to-Many but not for a One-to-Many.
The foreign key represents the link or association to the parent entity on the 'One' side.

question: Describe the comprehensive steps required to map a complex ER model with specialization/generalization (using the overlapping and total constraints) to a relational schema. Compare and contrast at least two different mapping options and justify the chosen approach for this specific scenario.
expectedPoints: For a total specialization (every superclass instance must be a subclass instance), a common approach is to create a relation for the superclass and each subclass, with the subclass relations containing only their specific attributes and a primary key that also serves as a foreign key referencing the superclass.

For overlapping subclasses (an instance can belong to more than one subclass), creating separate relations for each subclass is necessary to avoid NULL values and model the multiple memberships correctly.

An alternative option, creating a single relation with all attributes and a type flag, is rejected for overlapping constraints as it would lead to excessive NULL values for attributes not applicable to all instances.

The justification hinges on accurately representing the business rules: the total constraint ensures completeness, and the overlapping constraint dictates the need for multiple, separate foreign key relationships from the subclasses to the superclass.
